todos.content: Step 1: Orient in codebase and locate Relation and shared pieces
Step 2: Map Relation contract and included modules with file:line
Step 3: Trace query build-up and execution lifecycle
Step 4: Audit every dependent of Relation contract across repo
Step 5: Trace relation behavior reuse for associations
Step 6: Assess blast radius ranked by risk

argument: 123: # 124: # user = users.new { |user| user.name = 'Oscar' } 125: # user.name # => Oscar 126: def new(attributes = nil, &block) 127: if attributes.is_a?(Array) 128: attributes.collect { |attr| new(attr, &block) } 129: else 130: block = current_scope_restoring_block(&block) 131: scoping { _new(attributes, &block) } 132: end 133: end 134: alias build new 135: 136: # Tries to create a new record with the same scoped attributes 137: # defined in the relation. Returns the initialized object if validation fails. 138: # 139: # Expects arguments in the same format as 140: # {ActiveRecord::Base.create}[rdoc-ref:Persistence::ClassMethods#create]. 141: # 142: # ==== Examples 143: # 144: # users = User.where(name: 'Oscar') 145: # users.create # => # 146: # 147: # users.create(name: 'fxn') 148: # users.create # => # 149: # 150: # users.create { |user| user.name = 'tenderlove' } 151: # # => # 152: # 153: # users.create(name: nil) # validation on name 154: # # => # 155: def create(attributes = nil, &block) 156: if attributes.is_a?(Array) 157: attributes.collect { |attr| create(attr, &block) } 158: else 159: block = current_scope_restoring_block(&block) 160: scoping { _create(attributes, &block) } 161: end 162: end 163: 164: # Similar to #create, but calls 165: # {create!}[rdoc-ref:Persistence::ClassMethods#create!] 166: # on the base class. Raises an exception if a validation error occurs. 167: # 168: # Expects arguments in the same format as 169: # {ActiveRecord::Base.create!}[rdoc-ref:Persistence::ClassMethods#create!]. 170: def create!(attributes = nil, &block) 171: if attributes.is_a?(Array) 172: attributes.collect { |attr| create!(attr, &block) } 173: else 174: block = current_scope_restoring_block(&block) 175: scoping { _create!(attributes, &block) } 176: end 177: end 178: 179: def first_or_create(attributes = nil, &block) # :nodoc: 180: first || create(attributes, &block) 181: end 182: 183: def first_or_create!(attributes = nil, &block) # :nodoc: 184: first || create!(attributes, &block) 185: end 186: 187: def first_or_initialize(attributes = nil, &block) # :nodoc: 188: first || new(attributes, &block) 189: end 190: 191: # Finds the first record with the given attributes, or creates a record 192: # with the attributes if one is not found: 193: # 194: # # Find the first user named "Penélope" or create a new one. 195: # User.find_or_create_by(first_name: 'Penélope') 196: # # => # 197: # 198: # # Find the first user named "Penélope" or create a new one. 199: # # We already have one so the existing record will be returned. 200: # User.find_or_create_by(first_name: 'Penélope') 201: # # => # 202: # 203: # # Find the first user named "Scarlett" or create a new one with 204: # # a particular last name. 205: # User.create_with(last_name: 'Johansson').find_or_create_by(first_name: 'Scarlett') 206: # # => # 207: # 208: # This method accepts a block, which is passed down to #create. The last example 209: # above can be alternatively written this way: 210: # 211: # # Find the first user named "Scarlett" or create a new one with a 212: # # particular last name. 213: # User.find_or_create_by(first_name: 'Scarlett') do |user| 214: # user.last_name = 'Johansson' 215: # end 216: # # => # 217: # 218: # This method always returns a record, but if creation was attempted and 219: # failed due to validation errors it won't be persisted, you get what 220: # #create returns in such situation. 221: # 222: # If creation failed because of a unique constraint, this method will 223: # assume it encountered a race condition and will try finding the record 224: # once more. If somehow the second find still does not find a record 225: # because a concurrent DELETE happened, it will then raise an 226: # ActiveRecord::RecordNotFound exception. 227: # 228: # Please note this method is not atomic, it runs first a SELECT, 229: # and if there are no results an INSERT is attempted. So if the table 230: # doesn't have a relevant unique constraint it could be the case that 231: # you end up with two or more similar records. 232: def find_or_create_by(attributes, &block) 233: find_by(attributes) || create_or_find_by(attributes, &block) 234: end 235: 236: # Like #find_or_create_by, but calls 237: # {create!}[rdoc-ref:Persistence::ClassMethods#create!] so an exception 238: # is raised if the created record is invalid. 239: def find_or_create_by!(attributes, &block) 240: find_by(attributes) || create_or_find_by!(attributes, &block) 241: end 242: 243: # Attempts to create a record with the given attributes in a table that has a unique database constraint 244: # on one or several of its columns. If a row already exists with one or several of these 245: # unique constraints, the exception such an insertion would normally raise is caught, 246: # and the existing record with those attributes is found using #find_by!. 247: # 248: # This is similar to #find_or_create_by, but tries to create the record first. As such it is 249: # better suited for cases where the record is most likely not to exist yet. 250: # 251: # There are several drawbacks to #create_or_find_by, though: 252: # 253: # * The underlying table must have the relevant columns defined with unique database constraints. 254: # * A unique constraint violation may be triggered by only one, or at least less than all, 255: # of the given attributes. This means that the subsequent #find_by! may fail to find a 256: # matching record, which will then raise an ActiveRecord::RecordNotFound exception, 257: # rather than a record with the given attributes. 258: # * While we avoid the race condition between SELECT -> INSERT from #find_or_create_by, 259: # we actually have another race condition between INSERT -> SELECT, which can be triggered 260: # if a DELETE between those two statements is run by another client. But for most applications, 261: # that's a significantly less likely condition to hit. 262: # * It relies on exception handling to handle control flow, which may be marginally slower. 263: # * The primary key may auto-increment on each create, even if it fails. This can accelerate 264: # the problem of running out of integers, if the underlying table is still stuck on a primary 265: # key of type int (note: All \Rails apps since 5.1+ have defaulted to bigint, which is not liable 266: # to this problem). 267: # * Columns with unique database constraints should not have uniqueness validations defined, 268: # otherwise #create will fail due to validation errors and #find_by will never be called. 269: # 270: # This method will return a record if all given attributes are covered by unique constraints 271: # (unless the INSERT -> DELETE -> SELECT race condition is triggered), but if creation was attempted 272: # and failed due to validation errors it won't be persisted, you get what #create returns in 273: # such situation. 274: def create_or_find_by(attributes, &block) 275: with_connection do |connection| 276: record = nil 277: transaction(requires_new: true) do 278: record = create(attributes, &block) 279: record._last_transaction_return_status || raise(ActiveRecord::Rollback) 280: end 281: record 282: rescue ActiveRecord::RecordNotUnique 283: if connection.transaction_open? 284: rewhere(attributes).lock.take! 285: else 286: find_by!(attributes) 287: end 288: end 289: end 290: 291: # Like #create_or_find_by, but calls 292: # {create!}[rdoc-ref:Persistence::ClassMethods#create!] so an exception 293: # is raised if the created record is invalid. 294: def create_or_find_by!(attributes, &block) 295: with_connection do |connection| 296: record = nil 297: transaction(requires_new: true) do 298: record = create!(attributes, &block) 299: record._last_transaction_return_status || raise(ActiveRecord::Rollback) 300: end 301: record 302: rescue ActiveRecord::RecordNotUnique 303: if connection.transaction_open? 304: rewhere(attributes).lock.take! 305: else 306: find_by!(attributes) 307: end 308: end 309: end 310: 311: # Like #find_or_create_by, but calls {new}[rdoc-ref:Core.new] 312: # instead of {create}[rdoc-ref:Persistence::ClassMethods#create]. 313: def find_or_initialize_by(attributes, &block) 314: find_by(attributes) || new(attributes, &block) 315: end 316: 317: # Runs EXPLAIN on the query or queries triggered by this relation and 318: # returns the result as a string. The string is formatted imitating the 319: # ones printed by the database shell. 320: # 321: # User.all.explain 322: # # EXPLAIN SELECT `users`.* FROM `users` 323: # # ... 324: # 325: # Note that this method actually runs the queries, since the results of some 326: # are needed by the next ones when eager loading is going on. 327: # 328: # To run EXPLAIN on queries created by +first+, +pluck+ and +count+, call 329: # these methods on +explain+: 330: # 331: # User.all.explain.count 332: # # EXPLAIN SELECT COUNT(*) FROM `users` 333: # # ... 334: # 335: # The column name can be passed if required: 336: # 337: # User.all.explain.maximum(:id) 338: # # EXPLAIN SELECT MAX(`users`.`id`) FROM `users` 339: # # ... 340: # 341: # Please see further details in the 342: # {Active Record Query Interface guide}[https://guides.rubyonrails.org/active_record_querying.html#running-explain]. 343: def explain(*options) 344: ExplainProxy.new(self, options) 345: end 346: 347: # Converts relation objects to Array. 348: def to_ary 349: records.dup 350: end 351: alias to_a to_ary 352: 353: def records # :nodoc: 354: load 355: @records 356: end 357: 358: # Serializes the relation objects Array. 359: def encode_with(coder) 360: coder.represent_seq(nil, records) 361: end 362: 363: # Returns size of the records. 364: def size 365: if loaded? 366: records.length 367: else 368: count(:all) 369: end 370: end 371: 372: # Returns true if there are no records. 373: def empty? 374: return true if @none 375: 376: if loaded? 377: records.empty? 378: else 379: !exists? 380: end 381: end 382: 383: # Returns true if there are no records. 384: # 385: # When a pattern argument is given, this method checks whether elements in 386: # the Enumerable match the pattern via the case-equality operator (===). 387: # 388: # posts.none?(Comment) # => true or false 389: def none?(*args) 390: return true if @none 391: 392: return super if args.present? || block_given? 393: empty? 394: end 395: 396: # Returns true if there are any records. 397: # 398: # When a pattern argument is given, this method checks whether elements in 399: # the Enumerable match the pattern via the case-equality operator (===). 400: # 401: # posts.any?(Post) # => true or false 402: def any?(*args) 403: return false if @none 404: 405: return super if args.present? || block_given? 406: !empty? 407: end 408: 409: # Returns true if there is exactly one record. 410: # 411: # When a pattern argument is given, this method checks whether elements in 412: # the Enumerable match the pattern via the case-equality operator (===). 413: # 414: # posts.one?(Post) # => true or false 415: def one?(*args) 416: return false if @none 417: 418: return super if args.present? || block_given? 419: return records.one? if loaded? 420: limited_count == 1 421: end 422: 423: # Returns true if there is more than one record. 424: def many? 425: return false if @none 426: 427: return super if block_given? 428: return records.many? if loaded? 429: limited_count > 1 430: end 431: 432: # Returns a stable cache key that can be used to identify this query. 433: # The cache key is built with a fingerprint of the SQL query. 434: # 435: # Product.where("name like ?", "%Cosmic Encounter%").cache_key 436: # # => "products/query-1850ab3d302391b85b8693e941286659" 437: # 438: # If ActiveRecord::Base.collection_cache_versioning is turned off, as it was 439: # in \Rails 6.0 and earlier, the cache key will also include a version. 440: # 441: # ActiveRecord::Base.collection_cache_versioning = false 442: # Product.where("name like ?", "%Cosmic Encounter%").cache_key 443: # # => "products/query-1850ab3d302391b85b8693e941286659-1-20150714212553907087000" 444: # 445: # You can also pass a custom timestamp column to fetch the timestamp of the 446: # last updated record. 447: # 448: # Product.where("name like ?", "%Game%").cache_key(:last_reviewed_at) 449: def cache_key(timestamp_column = "updated_at") 450: @cache_keys ||= {} 451: @cache_keys[timestamp_column] ||= model.collection_cache_key(self, timestamp_column) 452: end 453: 454: def compute_cache_key(timestamp_column = :updated_at) # :nodoc: 455: query_signature = ActiveSupport::Digest.hexdigest(to_sql) 456: key = "#{model.model_name.cache_key}/query-#{query_signature}" 457: 458: if model.collection_cache_versioning 459: key 460: else 461: "#{key}-#{compute_cache_version(timestamp_column)}" 462: end 463: end 464: private :compute_cache_key 465: 466: # Returns a cache version that can be used together with the cache key to form 467: # a recyclable caching scheme. The cache version is built with the number of records 468: # matching the query, and the timestamp of the last updated record. When a new record 469: # comes to match the query, or any of the existing records is updated or deleted, 470: # the cache version changes. 471: # 472: # If the collection is loaded, the method will iterate through the records 473: # to generate the timestamp, otherwise it will trigger one SQL query like: 474: # 475: # SELECT COUNT(*), MAX("products"."updated_at") FROM "products" WHERE (name like '%Cosmic Encounter%') 476: def cache_version(timestamp_column = :updated_at) 477: if model.collection_cache_versioning 478: @cache_versions ||= {} 479: @cache_versions[timestamp_column] ||= compute_cache_version(timestamp_column) 480: end 481: end 482: 483: def compute_cache_version(timestamp_column) # :nodoc: 484: timestamp_column = timestamp_column.to_s 485: 486: if loaded? 487: size = records.size 488: if size > 0 489: timestamp = records.map { |record| record.read_attribute(timestamp_column) }.max 490: end 491: else 492: collection = eager_loading? ? apply_join_dependency : self 493: 494: with_connection do |c| 495: column = c.visitor.compile(table[timestamp_column]) 496: select_values = "COUNT(*) AS #{model.adapter_class.quote_column_name("size")}, MAX(%s) AS timestamp" 497: 498: if collection.has_limit_or_offset? 499: query = collection.select("#{column} AS collection_cache_key_timestamp") 500: query._select!(table[Arel.star]) if distinct_value && collection.select_values.empty? 501: subquery_alias = "subquery_for_cache_key" 502: subquery_column = "#{subquery_alias}.collection_cache_key_timestamp" 503: arel = query.build_subquery(subquery_alias, select_values % subquery_column) 504: else 505: query = collection.unscope(:order) 506: query.select_values = [select_values % column] 507: arel = query.arel 508: end 509: 510: size, timestamp = c.select_rows(arel, nil).first 511: 512: if size 513: column_type = model.type_for_attribute(timestamp_column) 514: timestamp = column_type.deserialize(timestamp) 515: else 516: size = 0 517: end 518: end 519: end 520: 521: if timestamp 522: "#{size}-#{timestamp.utc.to_fs(model.cache_timestamp_format)}" 523: else 524: "#{size}" 525: end 526: end 527: private :compute_cache_version 528: 529: # Returns a cache key along with the version. 530: def cache_key_with_version 531: if version = cache_version 532: "#{cache_key}-#{version}" 533: else 534: cache_key 535: end 536: end 537: 538: # Scope all queries to the current scope. 539: # 540: # Comment.where(post_id: 1).scoping do 541: # Comment.first 542: # end 543: # # SELECT "comments".* FROM "comments" WHERE "comments"."post_id" = 1 ORDER BY "comments"."id" ASC LIMIT 1 544: # 545: # If all_queries: true is passed, scoping will apply to all queries 546: # for the relation including +update+ and +delete+ on instances. 547: # Once +all_queries+ is set to true it cannot be set to false in a 548: # nested block. 549: # 550: # Please check unscoped if you want to remove all previous scopes (including 551: # the default_scope) during the execution of a block. 552: def scoping(all_queries: nil, &block) 553: registry = model.scope_registry 554: if global_scope?(registry) && all_queries == false 555: raise ArgumentError, "Scoping is set to apply to all queries and cannot be unset in a nested block." 556: elsif already_in_scope?(registry) 557: yield 558: else 559: _scoping(self, registry, all_queries, &block) 560: end 561: end 562: 563: def _exec_scope(...) # :nodoc: 564: @delegate_to_model = true 565: registry = model.scope_registry 566: _scoping(nil, registry) { instance_exec(...) || self } 567: ensure 568: @delegate_to_model = false 569: end 570: 571: # Updates all records in the current relation with details given. This method constructs a single SQL UPDATE 572: # statement and sends it straight to the database. It does not instantiate the involved models and it does not 573: # trigger Active Record callbacks or validations. However, values passed to #update_all will still go through 574: # Active Record's normal type casting and serialization. Returns the number of rows affected. 575: # 576: # Note: As Active Record callbacks are not triggered, this method will not automatically update +updated_at+/+updated_on+ columns. 577: # 578: # ==== Parameters 579: # 580: # * +updates+ - A string, array, or hash representing the SET part of an SQL statement. Any strings provided will 581: # be type cast, unless you use +Arel.sql+. (Don't pass user-provided values to +Arel.sql+.) 582: # 583: # ==== Examples 584: # 585: # # Update all customers with the given attributes 586: # Customer.update_all wants_email: true 587: # 588: # # Update all books with 'Rails' in their title 589: # Book.where('title LIKE ?', '%Rails%').update_all(author: 'David') 590: # 591: # # Update all books that match conditions, but limit it to 5 ordered by date 592: # Book.where('title LIKE ?', '%Rails%').order(:created_at).limit(5).update_all(author: 'David') 593: # 594: # # Update all invoices and set the number column to its id value. 595: # Invoice.update_all('number = id') 596: # 597: # # Update all books with 'Rails' in their title 598: # Book.where('title LIKE ?', '%Rails%').update_all(title: Arel.sql("title + ' - volume 1'")) 599: def update_all(updates) 600: raise ArgumentError, "Empty list of attributes to change" if updates.blank? 601: 602: return 0 if @none 603: 604: invalid_methods = INVALID_METHODS_FOR_UPDATE_AND_DELETE_ALL.select do |method| 605: value = @values[method] 606: method == :distinct ? value : value&.any? 607: end 608: if invalid_methods.any? 609: ActiveRecord.deprecator.warn <<~MESSAGE 610: `#{invalid_methods.join(', ')}` is not supported by `update_all` and was never included in the generated query. 611: 612: Calling `#{invalid_methods.join(', ')}` with `update_all` will raise an error in Rails 8.2. 613: MESSAGE 614: end 615: 616: if updates.is_a?(Hash) 617: if model.locking_enabled? && 618: !updates.key?(model.locking_column) && 619: !updates.key?(model.locking_column.to_sym) 620: attr = table[model.locking_column] 621: updates[attr.name] = _increment_attribute(attr) 622: end 623: values = _substitute_values(updates) 624: else 625: values = Arel.sql(model.sanitize_sql_for_assignment(updates, table.name)) 626: end 627: 628: model.with_connection do |c| 629: arel = eager_loading? ? apply_join_dependency.arel : arel() 630: arel.source.left = table 631: 632: key = if model.composite_primary_key? 633: primary_key.map { |pk| table[pk] } 634: else 635: table[primary_key] 636: end 637: stmt = arel.compile_update(values, key) 638: c.update(stmt, "#{model} Update All").tap { reset } 639: end 640: end 641: 642: def update(id = :all, attributes) # :nodoc: 643: if id == :all 644: each { |record| record.update(attributes) } 645: else 646: model.update(id, attributes) 647: end 648: end 649: 650: def update!(id = :all, attributes) # :nodoc: 651: if id == :all 652: each { |record| record.update!(attributes) } 653: else 654: model.update!(id, attributes) 655: end 656: end 657: 658: 659: # Inserts a single record into the database in a single SQL INSERT 660: # statement. It does not instantiate any models nor does it trigger 661: # Active Record callbacks or validations. Though passed values 662: # go through Active Record's type casting and serialization. 663: # 664: # See #insert_all for documentation. 665: def insert(attributes, returning: nil, unique_by: nil, record_timestamps: nil) 666: insert_all([ attributes ], returning: returning, unique_by: unique_by, record_timestamps: record_timestamps) 667: end 668: 669: # Inserts multiple records into the database in a single SQL INSERT 670: # statement. It does not instantiate any models nor does it trigger 671: # Active Record callbacks or validations. Though passed values 672: # go through Active Record's type casting and serialization. 673: # 674: # The +attributes+ parameter is an Array of Hashes. Every Hash determines 675: # the attributes for a single row and must have the same keys. 676: # 677: # Rows are considered to be unique by every unique index on the table. Any 678: # duplicate rows are skipped. 679: # Override with :unique_by (see below). 680: # 681: # Returns an ActiveRecord::Result with its contents based on 682: # :returning (see below). 683: # 684: # ==== Options 685: # 686: # [:returning] 687: # (PostgreSQL, SQLite3, and MariaDB only) An array of attributes to return for all successfully 688: # inserted records, which by default is the primary key. 689: # Pass returning: %w[ id name ] for both id and name 690: # or returning: false to omit the underlying RETURNING SQL 691: # clause entirely. 692: # 693: # You can also pass an SQL string if you need more control on the return values 694: # (for example, returning: Arel.sql("id, name as new_name")). 695: # 696: # [:unique_by] 697: # (PostgreSQL and SQLite only) By default rows are considered to be unique 698: # by every unique index on the table. Any duplicate rows are skipped. 699: # 700: # To skip rows according to just one unique index pass :unique_by. 701: # 702: # Consider a Book model where no duplicate ISBNs make sense, but if any 703: # row has an existing id, or is not unique by another unique index, 704: # ActiveRecord::RecordNotUnique is raised. 705: # 706: # Unique indexes can be identified by columns or name: 707: # 708: # unique_by: :isbn 709: # unique_by: %i[ author_id name ] 710: # unique_by: :index_books_on_isbn 711: # 712: # [:record_timestamps] 713: # By default, automatic setting of timestamp columns is controlled by 714: # the model's record_timestamps config, matching typical 715: # behavior. 716: # 717: # To override this and force automatic setting of timestamp columns one 718: # way or the other, pass :record_timestamps: 719: # 720: # record_timestamps: true # Always set timestamps automatically 721: # record_timestamps: false # Never set timestamps automatically 722: # 723: # Because it relies on the index information from the database 724: # :unique_by is recommended to be paired with 725: # Active Record's schema_cache. 726: # 727: # ==== Example 728: # 729: # # Insert records and skip inserting any duplicates. 730: # # Here "Eloquent Ruby" is skipped because its id is not unique. 731: # 732: # Book.insert_all([ 733: # { id: 1, title: "Rework", author: "David" }, 734: # { id: 1, title: "Eloquent Ruby", author: "Russ" } 735: # ]) 736: # 737: # # insert_all works on chained scopes, and you can use create_with 738: # # to set default attributes for all inserted records. 739: # 1408:         registry.set_current_scope(model, previous)
1409:         if all_queries
1410:           registry.set_global_current_scope(model, previous_global)
1411:         end
1412:       end
1413: 
1414:       def _substitute_values(values)
1415:         values.map do |name, value|
1416:           attr = table[name]
1417:           if Arel.arel_node?(value)
1418:             if value.is_a?(Arel::Nodes::SqlLiteral)
1419:               value = Arel::Nodes::Grouping.new(value)
1420:             end
1421:           else
1422:             type = model.type_for_attribute(attr.name)
1423:             value = predicate_builder.build_bind_attribute(attr.name, type.cast(value))
1424:           end
1425:           [attr, value]
1426:         end
1427:       end
1428: 
1429:       def _increment_attribute(attribute, value = 1)
1430:         bind = predicate_builder.build_bind_attribute(attribute.name, value.abs)
1431:         expr = table.coalesce(attribute, 0)
1432:         expr = value < 0 ? expr - bind : expr + bind
1433:         expr.expr
1434:       end
1435: 
1436:       def exec_queries(&block)
1437:         if lock_value && model.current_preventing_writes
1438:           raise ActiveRecord::ReadOnlyError, "Lock query attempted while in readonly mode"
1439:         end
1440: 
1441:         skip_query_cache_if_necessary do
1442:           rows = if scheduled?
1443:             future = @future_result
1444:             @future_result = nil
1445:             future.result
1446:           else
1447:             exec_main_query
1448:           end
1449: 
1450:           records = instantiate_records(rows, &block)
1451:           preload_associations(records) unless skip_preloading_value
1452: 
1453:           records.each(&:readonly!) if readonly_value
1454:           records.each { |record| record.strict_loading!(strict_loading_value) } unless strict_loading_value.nil?
1455: 
1456:           records
1457:         end
1458:       end
1459: 
1460:       def exec_main_query(async: false)
1461:         if @none
1462:           if async
1463:             return FutureResult.wrap([])
1464:           else
1465:             return []
1466:           end
1467:         end
1468: 
1469:         skip_query_cache_if_necessary do
1470:           if where_clause.contradiction?
1471:             [].freeze
1472:           elsif eager_loading?
1473:             model.with_connection do |c|
1474:               apply_join_dependency do |relation, join_dependency|
1475:                 if relation.null_relation?
1476:                   [].freeze
1477:                 else
1478:                   relation = join_dependency.apply_column_aliases(relation)
1479:                   @_join_dependency = join_dependency
1480:                   c.select_all(relation.arel, "#{model.name} Eager Load", async: async)
1481:                 end
1482:               end
1483:             end
1484:           else
1485:             model.with_connection do |c|
1486:               model._query_by_sql(c, arel, async: async)
1487:             end
1488:           end
1489:         end
1490:       end
1491: 
1492:       def instantiate_records(rows, &block)
1493:         return [].freeze if rows.empty?
1494:         if eager_loading?
1495:           records = @_join_dependency.instantiate(rows, strict_loading_value, &block).freeze
1496:           @_join_dependency = nil
1497:           records
1498:         else
1499:           model._load_from_sql(rows, &block).freeze
1500:         end
1501:       end
1502: 
1503:       def skip_query_cache_if_necessary(&block)
1504:         if skip_query_cache_value
1505:           model.uncached(&block)
1506:         else
1507:           yield
1508:         end
1509:       end
1510: 
1511:       def references_eager_loaded_tables?
1512:         joined_tables = build_joins([]).flat_map do |join|
1513:           if join.is_a?(Arel::Nodes::StringJoin)
1514:             tables_in_string(join.left)
1515:           else
1516:             join.left.name
1517:           end
1518:         end
1519: 
1520:         joined_tables << table.name
1521: 
1522:         # always convert table names to downcase as in Oracle quoted table names are in uppercase
1523:         joined_tables.map!(&:downcase)
1524: 
1525:         !(references_values.map(&:to_s) - joined_tables).empty?
1526:       end
1527: 
1528:       def tables_in_string(string)
1529:         return [] if string.blank?
1530:         # always convert table names to downcase as in Oracle quoted table names are in uppercase
1531:         # ignore raw_sql_ that is used by Oracle adapter as alias for limit/offset subqueries
1532:         string.scan(/[a-zA-Z_][.\w]+(?=.?\.)/).map!(&:downcase) - ["raw_sql_"]
1533:       end
1534: 
1535:       def limited_count
1536:         limit_value ? count : limit(2).count
1537:       end
1538:   end
1539: end

1: # frozen_string_literal: true
2: 
3: require "active_support/core_ext/hash/except"
4: require "active_support/core_ext/hash/slice"
5: require "active_record/relation/merger"
6: 
7: module ActiveRecord
8:   module SpawnMethods
9:     def spawn # :nodoc:
10:       already_in_scope?(model.scope_registry) ? model.all : clone
11:     end
12: 
13:     # Merges in the conditions from <tt>other</tt>, if <tt>other</tt> is an ActiveRecord::Relation.
14:     # Returns an array representing the intersection of the resulting records with <tt>other</tt>, if <tt>other</tt> is an array.
15:     #
16:     #   Post.where(published: true).joins(:comments).merge( Comment.where(spam: false) )
17:     #   # Performs a single join query with both where conditions.
18:     #
19:     #   recent_posts = Post.order('created_at DESC').first(5)
20:     #   Post.where(published: true).merge(recent_posts)
21:     #   # Returns the intersection of all published posts with the 5 most recently created posts.
22:     #   # (This is just an example. You'd probably want to do this with a single query!)
23:     #
24:     # Procs will be evaluated by merge:
25:     #
26:     #   Post.where(published: true).merge(-> { joins(:comments) })
27:     #   # => Post.where(published: true).joins(:comments)
28:     #
29:     # This is mainly intended for sharing common conditions between multiple associations.
30:     #
31:     # For conditions that exist in both relations, those from <tt>other</tt> will take precedence.
32:     # To find the intersection of two relations, use QueryMethods#and.
33:     def merge(other, *rest)
34:       if other.is_a?(Array)
35:         records & other
36:       elsif other
37:         spawn.merge!(other, *rest)
38:       else
39:         raise ArgumentError, "invalid argument: #{other.inspect}."
40:       end
41:     end
42: 
43:     def merge!(other, *rest) # :nodoc:
44:       if other.is_a?(Hash)
45:         Relation::HashMerger.new(self, other).merge
46:       elsif other.is_a?(Relation)
47:         Relation::Merger.new(self, other).merge
48:       elsif other.respond_to?(:to_proc)
49:         instance_exec(&other)
50:       else
51:         raise ArgumentError, "#{other.inspect} is not an ActiveRecord::Relation"
52:       end
53:     end
54: 
55:     # Removes the condition(s) specified in +skips+ from the query.
56:     #
57:     #   Post.order('id asc').except(:order)                  # removes the order condition
58:     #   Post.where('id > 10').order('id asc').except(:where) # removes the where condition but keeps the order
59:     def except(*skips)
60:       relation_with values.except(*skips)
61:     end
62: 
63:     # Keeps only the condition(s) specified in +onlies+ in the query, removing all others.
64:     #
65:     #   Post.order('id asc').only(:where)         # keeps only the where condition, removes the order
66:     #   Post.order('id asc').only(:where, :order) # keeps only the where and order conditions
67:     def only(*onlies)
68:       relation_with values.slice(*onlies)
69:     end
70: 
71:     private
72:       def relation_with(values)
73:         result = spawn
74:         result.instance_variable_set(:@values, values)
75:         result
76:       end
77:   end
78: end

1: # frozen_string_literal: true
2: 
3: 
4: module ActiveRecord
5:   module Delegation # :nodoc:
6:     class << self
7:       def delegated_classes
8:         [
9:           ActiveRecord::Relation,
10:           ActiveRecord::Associations::CollectionProxy,
11:           ActiveRecord::AssociationRelation,
12:           ActiveRecord::DisableJoinsAssociationRelation,
13:         ]
14:       end
15: 
16:       def uncacheable_methods
17:         @uncacheable_methods ||= (
18:           delegated_classes.flat_map(&:public_instance_methods) - ActiveRecord::Relation.public_instance_methods
19:         ).to_set.freeze
20:       end
21:     end
22: 
23:     module DelegateCache # :nodoc:
24:       @delegate_base_methods = true
25:       singleton_class.attr_accessor :delegate_base_methods
26: 
27:       def relation_delegate_class(klass)
28:         @relation_delegate_cache[klass]
29:       end
30: 
31:       def initialize_relation_delegate_cache
32:         @relation_delegate_cache = cache = {}
33:         Delegation.delegated_classes.each do |klass|
34:           delegate = Class.new(klass) {
35:             include ClassSpecificRelation
36:           }
37:           include_relation_methods(delegate)
38:           mangled_name = klass.name.gsub("::", "_")
39:           const_set mangled_name, delegate
40:           private_constant mangled_name
41: 
42:           cache[klass] = delegate
43:         end
44:         @relation_delegate_cache.freeze
45:       end
46: 
47:       def inherited(child_class)
48:         child_class.initialize_relation_delegate_cache
49:         super
50:       end
51: 
52:       def generate_relation_method(method)
53:         generated_relation_methods.generate_method(method)
54:       end
55: 
56:       protected
57:         def include_relation_methods(delegate)
58:           superclass.include_relation_methods(delegate) unless base_class?
59:           delegate.include generated_relation_methods
60:         end
61: 
62:       private
63:         def generated_relation_methods
64:           @generated_relation_methods ||= GeneratedRelationMethods.new.tap do |mod|
65:             const_set(:GeneratedRelationMethods, mod)
66:             private_constant :GeneratedRelationMethods
67:           end
68:         end
69:     end
70: 
71:     class GeneratedRelationMethods < Module # :nodoc:
72:       MUTEX = Mutex.new
73: 
74:       def generate_method(method)
75:         MUTEX.synchronize do
76:           return if method_defined?(method)
77: 
78:           if /\A[a-zA-Z_]\w*[!?]?\z/.match?(method) && !::ActiveSupport::Delegation::RESERVED_METHOD_NAMES.include?(method.to_s)
79:             module_eval <<-RUBY, __FILE__, __LINE__ + 1
80:               def #{method}(...)
81:                 scoping { model.#{method}(...) }
82:               end
83:             RUBY
84:           else
85:             define_method(method) do |*args, **kwargs, &block|
86:               scoping { model.public_send(method, *args, **kwargs, &block) }
87:             end
88:           end
89:         end
90:       end
91:     end
92:     private_constant :GeneratedRelationMethods
93: 
94:     extend ActiveSupport::Concern
95: 
96:     # This module creates compiled delegation methods dynamically at runtime, which makes
97:     # subsequent calls to that method faster by avoiding method_missing. The delegations
98:     # may vary depending on the model of a relation, so we create a subclass of Relation
99:     # for each different model, and the delegations are compiled into that subclass only.
100: 
101:     delegate :to_xml, :encode_with, :length, :each, :join, :intersect?,
102:              :[], :&, :|, :+, :-, :sample, :reverse, :rotate, :compact, :in_groups, :in_groups_of,
103:              :to_sentence, :to_fs, :to_formatted_s, :as_json,
104:              :shuffle, :split, :slice, :index, :rindex, to: :records
105: 
106:     delegate :primary_key, :with_connection, :connection, :table_name, :transaction, :sanitize_sql_like, :unscoped, :name, to: :model
107: 
108:     module ClassSpecificRelation # :nodoc:
109:       extend ActiveSupport::Concern
110: 
111:       module ClassMethods # :nodoc:
112:         def name
113:           superclass.name
114:         end
115:       end
116: 
117:       private
118:         def method_missing(method, ...)
119:           if model.respond_to?(method)
120:             if !DelegateCache.delegate_base_methods && Base.respond_to?(method)
121:               # A common mistake in Active Record's own code is to call `ActiveRecord::Base`
122:               # class methods on Association. It works because it's automatically delegated, but
123:               # can introduce subtle bugs because it sets the global scope.
124:               # We can't deprecate this behavior because gems might depend on it, however we
125:               # can ban it from Active Record's own test suite to avoid regressions.
126:               raise NotImplementedError, "Active Record code shouldn't rely on association delegation into ActiveRecord::Base methods"
127:             elsif !Delegation.uncacheable_methods.include?(method)
128:               model.generate_relation_method(method)
129:             end
130: 
131:             scoping { model.public_send(method, ...) }
132:           else
133:             super
134:           end
135:         end
136:     end
137: 
138:     module ClassMethods # :nodoc:
139:       def create(model, ...)
140:         relation_class_for(model).new(model, ...)
141:       end
142: 
143:       private
144:         def relation_class_for(model)
145:           model.relation_delegate_class(self)
146:         end
147:     end
148: 
149:     private
150:       def respond_to_missing?(method, _)
151:         super || model.respond_to?(method)
152:       end
153:   end
154: end

1: # frozen_string_literal: true
2: 
3: require "active_support/core_ext/hash/keys"
4: 
5: module ActiveRecord
6:   class Relation
7:     class HashMerger # :nodoc:
8:       attr_reader :relation, :hash
9: 
10:       def initialize(relation, hash)
11:         hash.assert_valid_keys(*Relation::VALUE_METHODS)
12: 
13:         @relation = relation
14:         @hash     = hash
15:       end
16: 
17:       def merge
18:         Merger.new(relation, other).merge
19:       end
20: 
21:       # Applying values to a relation has some side effects. E.g.
22:       # interpolation might take place for where values. So we should
23:       # build a relation to merge in rather than directly merging
24:       # the values.
25:       def other
26:         other = Relation.create(
27:           relation.model,
28:           table: relation.table,
29:           predicate_builder: relation.predicate_builder
30:         )
31:         hash.each do |k, v|
32:           k = :_select if k == :select
33:           if Array === v
34:             other.public_send("#{k}!", *v)
35:           else
36:             other.public_send("#{k}!", v)
37:           end
38:         end
39:         other
40:       end
41:     end
42: 
43:     class Merger # :nodoc:
44:       attr_reader :relation, :values, :other
45: 
46:       def initialize(relation, other)
47:         @relation = relation
48:         @values   = other.values
49:         @other    = other
50:       end
51: 
52:       NORMAL_VALUES = (Relation::VALUE_METHODS - Relation::CLAUSE_METHODS -
53:                       [
54:                         :select, :includes, :preload, :joins, :left_outer_joins,
55:                         :order, :reverse_order, :lock, :create_with, :reordering
56:                       ]).freeze
57: 
58:       def merge
59:         NORMAL_VALUES.each do |name|
60:           value = values[name]
61:           # The unless clause is here mostly for performance reasons (since the `send` call might be moderately
62:           # expensive), most of the time the value is going to be `nil` or `.blank?`, the only catch is that
63:           # `false.blank?` returns `true`, so there needs to be an extra check so that explicit `false` values
64:           # don't fall through the cracks.
65:           unless value.nil? || (value.blank? && false != value)
66:             relation.public_send(:"#{name}!", *value)
67:           end
68:         end
69: 
70:         relation.none! if other.null_relation?
71: 
72:         merge_select_values
73:         merge_multi_values
74:         merge_single_values
75:         merge_clauses
76:         merge_preloads
77:         merge_joins
78:         merge_outer_joins
79: 
80:         relation
81:       end
82: 
83:       private
84:         def merge_select_values
85:           return if other.select_values.empty?
86: 
87:           if other.model == relation.model
88:             relation.select_values |= other.select_values
89:           else
90:             relation.select_values |= other.instance_eval do
91:               arel_columns(select_values)
92:             end
93:           end
94:         end
95: 
96:         def merge_preloads
97:           return if other.preload_values.empty? && other.includes_values.empty?
98: 
99:           if other.model == relation.model
100:             relation.preload_values |= other.preload_values unless other.preload_values.empty?
101:             relation.includes_values |= other.includes_values unless other.includes_values.empty?
102:           else
103:             reflection = relation.model.reflect_on_all_associations.find do |r|
104:               r.class_name == other.model.name
105:             end || return
106: 
107:             unless other.preload_values.empty?
108:               relation.preload! reflection.name => other.preload_values
109:             end
110: 
111:             unless other.includes_values.empty?
112:               relation.includes! reflection.name => other.includes_values
113:             end
114:           end
115:         end
116: 
117:         def merge_joins
118:           return if other.joins_values.empty?
119: 
120:           if other.model == relation.model
121:             relation.joins_values |= other.joins_values
122:           else
123:             associations, others = other.joins_values.partition do |join|
124:               case join
125:               when Hash, Symbol, Array; true
126:               end
127:             end
128: 
129:             join_dependency = other.construct_join_dependency(
130:               associations, Arel::Nodes::InnerJoin
131:             )
132:             relation.joins!(join_dependency, *others)
133:           end
134:         end
135: 
136:         def merge_outer_joins
137:           return if other.left_outer_joins_values.empty?
138: 
139:           if other.model == relation.model
140:             relation.left_outer_joins_values |= other.left_outer_joins_values
141:           else
142:             associations, others = other.left_outer_joins_values.partition do |join|
143:               case join
144:               when Hash, Symbol, Array; true
145:               end
146:             end
147: 
148:             join_dependency = other.construct_join_dependency(
149:               associations, Arel::Nodes::OuterJoin
150:             )
151:             relation.left_outer_joins!(join_dependency, *others)
152:           end
153:         end
154: 
155:         def merge_multi_values
156:           if other.reordering_value
157:             # override any order specified in the original relation
158:             relation.reorder!(*other.order_values)
159:           elsif other.order_values.any?
160:             # merge in order_values from relation
161:             relation.order!(*other.order_values)
162:           end
163: 
164:           relation.extending!(*other.extensions)
165:         end
166: 
167:         def merge_single_values
168:           relation.lock_value ||= other.lock_value if other.lock_value
169: 
170:           unless other.create_with_value.blank?
171:             relation.create_with_value = (relation.create_with_value || {}).merge(other.create_with_value)
172:           end
173:         end
174: 
175:         def merge_clauses
176:           relation.from_clause = other.from_clause if replace_from_clause?
177: 
178:           where_clause = relation.where_clause.merge(other.where_clause)
179:           relation.where_clause = where_clause unless where_clause.empty?
180: 
181:           having_clause = relation.having_clause.merge(other.having_clause)
182:           relation.having_clause = having_clause unless having_clause.empty?
183:         end
184: 
185:         def replace_from_clause?
186:           relation.from_clause.empty? && !other.from_clause.empty? &&
187:             relation.model.base_class == other.model.base_class
188:         end
189:     end
190:   end
191: end

26:     # Executes a custom SQL query against your database and returns all the results. The results will
27:     # be returned as an array, with the requested columns encapsulated as attributes of the model you call
28:     # this method from. For example, if you call <tt>Product.find_by_sql</tt>, then the results will be returned in
29:     # a +Product+ object with the attributes you specified in the SQL query.
30:     #
31:     # If you call a complicated SQL query which spans multiple tables, the columns specified by the
32:     # SELECT will be attributes of the model, whether or not they are columns of the corresponding
33:     # table.
34:     #
35:     # The +sql+ parameter is a full SQL query as a string. It will be called as is; there will be
36:     # no database agnostic conversions performed. This should be a last resort because using
37:     # database-specific terms will lock you into using that particular database engine, or require you to
38:     # change your call if you switch engines.
39:     #
40:     #   # A simple SQL query spanning multiple tables
41:     #   Post.find_by_sql "SELECT p.title, c.author FROM posts p, comments c WHERE p.id = c.post_id"
42:     #   # => [#<Post:0x36bff9c @attributes={"title"=>"Ruby Meetup", "author"=>"Quentin"}>, ...]
43:     #
44:     # You can use the same string replacement techniques as you can with ActiveRecord::QueryMethods#where :
45:     #
46:     #   Post.find_by_sql ["SELECT title FROM posts WHERE author = ? AND created > ?", author_id, start_date]
47:     #   Post.find_by_sql ["SELECT body FROM comments WHERE author = :user_id OR approved_by = :user_id", { :user_id => user_id }]
48:     #
49:     # Note that building your own SQL query string from user input {may expose your application to
50:     # injection attacks}[https://guides.rubyonrails.org/security.html#sql-injection].
51:     def find_by_sql(sql, binds = [], preparable: nil, allow_retry: false, &block)
52:       result = with_connection do |c|
53:         _query_by_sql(c, sql, binds, preparable: preparable, allow_retry: allow_retry)
54:       end
55:       _load_from_sql(result, &block)
56:     end
57: 
58:     # Same as #find_by_sql but perform the query asynchronously and returns an ActiveRecord::Promise.
59:     def async_find_by_sql(sql, binds = [], preparable: nil, allow_retry: false, &block)
60:       with_connection do |c|
61:         _query_by_sql(c, sql, binds, preparable: preparable, allow_retry: allow_retry, async: true)
62:       end.then do |result|
63:         _load_from_sql(result, &block)
64:       end
65:     end
66: 
67:     def _query_by_sql(connection, sql, binds = [], preparable: nil, async: false, allow_retry: false) # :nodoc:
68:       connection.select_all(sanitize_sql(sql), "#{name} Load", binds, preparable: preparable, async: async, allow_retry: allow_retry)
69:     end
70: 
71:     def _load_from_sql(result_set, &block) # :nodoc:
72:       return [] if result_set.empty?
73: 
74:       column_types = result_set.column_types
75: 
76:       unless column_types.empty?
77:         column_types = column_types.reject { |k, _| attribute_types.key?(k) }
78:       end
79: 
80:       message_bus = ActiveSupport::Notifications.instrumenter
81: 
82:       payload = {
83:         record_count: result_set.length,
84:         class_name: name
85:       }
86: 
87:       message_bus.instrument("instantiation.active_record", payload) do
88:         if result_set.includes_column?(inheritance_column)
89:           result_set.indexed_rows.map { |record| instantiate(record, column_types, &block) }
90:         else
91:           # Instantiate a homogeneous set
92:           result_set.indexed_rows.map { |record| instantiate_instance_of(self, record, column_types, &block) }
93:         end
94:       end
95:     end
96: 
97:     # Returns the result of an SQL statement that should only include a COUNT(*) in the SELECT part.
98:     # The use of this method should be restricted to complicated SQL queries that can't be executed
99:     # using the ActiveRecord::Calculations class methods. Look into those before using this method,
100:     # as it could lock you into a specific database engine or require a code change to switch
101:     # database engines.
102:     #
103:     #   Product.count_by_sql "SELECT COUNT(*) FROM sales s, customers c WHERE s.customer_id = c.id"
104:     #   # => 12
105:     #
106:     # ==== Parameters
107:     #
108:     # * +sql+ - An SQL statement which should return a count query from the database, see the example above.
109:     def count_by_sql(sql)
110:       with_connection do |c|
111:         c.select_value(sanitize_sql(sql), "#{name} Count").to_i
112:       end
113:     end
114: 
115:     # Same as #count_by_sql but perform the query asynchronously and returns an ActiveRecord::Promise.
116:     def async_count_by_sql(sql)
117:       with_connection do |c|
118:         c.select_value(sanitize_sql(sql), "#{name} Count", async: true).then(&:to_i)
119:       end
120:     end
121:   end
122: end

1: # frozen_string_literal: true
2: 
3: module ActiveRecord
4:   # = Active Record \Named \Scopes
5:   module Scoping
6:     module Named
7:       extend ActiveSupport::Concern
8: 
9:       module ClassMethods
10:         # Returns an ActiveRecord::Relation scope object.
11:         #
12:         #   posts = Post.all
13:         #   posts.size # Fires "select count(*) from  posts" and returns the count
14:         #   posts.each {|p| puts p.name } # Fires "select * from posts" and loads post objects
15:         #
16:         #   fruits = Fruit.all
17:         #   fruits = fruits.where(color: 'red') if options[:red_only]
18:         #   fruits = fruits.limit(10) if limited?
19:         #
20:         # You can define a scope that applies to all finders using
21:         # {default_scope}[rdoc-ref:Scoping::Default::ClassMethods#default_scope].
22:         def all(all_queries: nil)
23:           scope = current_scope
24: 
25:           if scope
26:             if self == scope.model
27:               scope.clone
28:             else
29:               relation.merge!(scope)
30:             end
31:           else
32:             default_scoped(all_queries: all_queries)
33:           end
34:         end
35: 
36:         def scope_for_association(scope = relation) # :nodoc:
37:           if current_scope&.empty_scope?
38:             scope
39:           else
40:             default_scoped(scope)
41:           end
42:         end
43: 
44:         # Returns a scope for the model with default scopes.
45:         def default_scoped(scope = relation, all_queries: nil)
46:           build_default_scope(scope, all_queries: all_queries) || scope
47:         end
48: 
49:         def default_extensions # :nodoc:
50:           if scope = scope_for_association || build_default_scope
51:             scope.extensions
52:           else
53:             []
54:           end
55:         end
56: 
57:         # Adds a class method for retrieving and querying objects.
58:         # The method is intended to return an ActiveRecord::Relation
59:         # object, which is composable with other scopes.
60:         # If it returns +nil+ or +false+, an
61:         # {all}[rdoc-ref:Scoping::Named::ClassMethods#all] scope is returned instead.
62:         #
63:         # A \scope represents a narrowing of a database query, such as
64:         # <tt>where(color: :red).select('shirts.*').includes(:washing_instructions)</tt>.
65:         #
66:         #   class Shirt < ActiveRecord::Base
67:         #     scope :red, -> { where(color: 'red') }
68:         #     scope :dry_clean_only, -> { joins(:washing_instructions).where('washing_instructions.dry_clean_only = ?', true) }
69:         #   end
70:         #
71:         # The above calls to #scope define class methods <tt>Shirt.red</tt> and
72:         # <tt>Shirt.dry_clean_only</tt>. <tt>Shirt.red</tt>, in effect,
73:         # represents the query <tt>Shirt.where(color: 'red')</tt>.
74:         #
75:         # Note that this is simply 'syntactic sugar' for defining an actual
76:         # class method:
77:         #
78:         #   class Shirt < ActiveRecord::Base
79:         #     def self.red
80:         #       where(color: 'red')
81:         #     end
82:         #   end
83:         #
84:         # Unlike <tt>Shirt.find(...)</tt>, however, the object returned by
85:         # <tt>Shirt.red</tt> is not an Array but an ActiveRecord::Relation,
86:         # which is composable with other scopes; it resembles the association object
87:         # constructed by a {has_many}[rdoc-ref:Associations::ClassMethods#has_many]
88:         # declaration. For instance, you can invoke <tt>Shirt.red.first</tt>, <tt>Shirt.red.count</tt>,
89:         # <tt>Shirt.red.where(size: 'small')</tt>. Also, just as with the
90:         # association objects, named \scopes act like an Array, implementing
91:         # Enumerable; <tt>Shirt.red.each(&block)</tt>, <tt>Shirt.red.first</tt>,
92:         # and <tt>Shirt.red.inject(memo, &block)</tt> all behave as if
93:         # <tt>Shirt.red</tt> really was an array.
94:         #
95:         # These named \scopes are composable. For instance,
96:         # <tt>Shirt.red.dry_clean_only</tt> will produce all shirts that are
97:         # both red and dry clean only. Nested finds and calculations also work
98:         # with these compositions: <tt>Shirt.red.dry_clean_only.count</tt>
99:         # returns the number of garments for which these criteria obtain.
100:         # Similarly with <tt>Shirt.red.dry_clean_only.average(:thread_count)</tt>.
101:         #
102:         # All scopes are available as class methods on the ActiveRecord::Base
103:         # descendant upon which the \scopes were defined. But they are also
104:         # available to {has_many}[rdoc-ref:Associations::ClassMethods#has_many]
105:         # associations. If,
106:         #
107:         #   class Person < ActiveRecord::Base
108:         #     has_many :shirts
109:         #   end
110:         #
111:         # then <tt>elton.shirts.red.dry_clean_only</tt> will return all of
112:         # Elton's red, dry clean only shirts.
113:         #
114:         # \Named scopes can also have extensions, just as with
115:         # {has_many}[rdoc-ref:Associations::ClassMethods#has_many] declarations:
116:         #
117:         #   class Shirt < ActiveRecord::Base
118:         #     scope :red, -> { where(color: 'red') } do
119:         #       def dom_id
120:         #         'red_shirts'
121:         #       end
122:         #     end
123:         #   end
124:         #
125:         # Scopes can also be used while creating/building a record.
126:         #
127:         #   class Article < ActiveRecord::Base
128:         #     scope :published, -> { where(published: true) }
129:         #   end
130:         #
131:         #   Article.published.new.published    # => true
132:         #   Article.published.create.published # => true
133:         #
134:         # \Class methods on your model are automatically available
135:         # on scopes. Assuming the following setup:
136:         #
137:         #   class Article < ActiveRecord::Base
138:         #     scope :published, -> { where(published: true) }
139:         #     scope :featured, -> { where(featured: true) }
140:         #
141:         #     def self.latest_article
142:         #       order('published_at desc').first
143:         #     end
144:         #
145:         #     def self.titles
146:         #       pluck(:title)
147:         #     end
148:         #   end
149:         #
150:         # We are able to call the methods like this:
151:         #
152:         #   Article.published.featured.latest_article
153:         #   Article.featured.titles
154:         def scope(name, body, &block)
155:           unless body.respond_to?(:call)
156:             raise ArgumentError, "The scope body needs to be callable."
157:           end
158: 
159:           if dangerous_class_method?(name)
160:             raise ArgumentError, "You tried to define a scope named \"#{name}\" " \
161:               "on the model \"#{self.name}\", but Active Record already defined " \
162:               "a class method with the same name."
163:           end
164: 
165:           if method_defined_within?(name, Relation)
166:             raise ArgumentError, "You tried to define a scope named \"#{name}\" " \
167:               "on the model \"#{self.name}\", but ActiveRecord::Relation already defined " \
168:               "an instance method with the same name."
169:           end
170: 
171:           extension = Module.new(&block) if block
172: 
173:           if body.respond_to?(:to_proc)
174:             singleton_class.define_method(name) do |*args|
175:               scope = all._exec_scope(*args, &body)
176:               scope = scope.extending(extension) if extension
177:               scope
178:             end
179:           else
180:             singleton_class.define_method(name) do |*args|
181:               scope = body.call(*args) || all
182:               scope = scope.extending(extension) if extension
183:               scope
184:             end
185:           end
186:           singleton_class.send(:ruby2_keywords, name)
187: 
188:           generate_relation_method(name)
189:         end
190: 
191:         private
192:           def singleton_method_added(name)
193:             super
194:             # Most Kernel extends are both singleton and instance methods so
195:             # respond_to is a fast check, but we don't want to define methods
196:             # only on the module (ex. Module#name)
197:             generate_relation_method(name) if Kernel.respond_to?(name) && (Kernel.method_defined?(name) || Kernel.private_method_defined?(name)) && !ActiveRecord::Relation.method_defined?(name)
198:           end
199:       end
200:     end
201:   end
202: end

264:     # Specify associations +args+ to be eager loaded using a <tt>LEFT OUTER JOIN</tt>.
265:     # Performs a single query joining all specified associations. For example:
266:     #
267:     #   users = User.eager_load(:address).limit(5)
268:     #   users.each do |user|
269:     #     user.address.city
270:     #   end
271:     #
272:     #   # SELECT "users"."id" AS t0_r0, "users"."name" AS t0_r1, ... FROM "users"
273:     #   #   LEFT OUTER JOIN "addresses" ON "addresses"."id" = "users"."address_id"
274:     #   #   LIMIT 5
275:     #
276:     # Instead of loading the 5 addresses with 5 separate queries, all addresses
277:     # are loaded with a single joined query.
278:     #
279:     # Loading multiple and nested associations is possible using Hashes and Arrays,
280:     # similar to #includes:
281:     #
282:     #   User.eager_load(:address, friends: [:address, :followers])
283:     #   # SELECT "users"."id" AS t0_r0, "users"."name" AS t0_r1, ... FROM "users"
284:     #   #   LEFT OUTER JOIN "addresses" ON "addresses"."id" = "users"."address_id"
285:     #   #   LEFT OUTER JOIN "friends" ON "friends"."user_id" = "users"."id"
286:     #   #   ...
287:     #
288:     # NOTE: Loading the associations in a join can result in many rows that
289:     # contain redundant data and it performs poorly at scale.
290:     def eager_load(*args)
291:       check_if_method_has_arguments!(__callee__, args)
292:       spawn.eager_load!(*args)
293:     end

300:     # Specify associations +args+ to be eager loaded using separate queries.
301:     # A separate query is performed for each association.

303:     #   users = User.preload(:address).limit(5)
304:     #   users.each do |user|
305:     #     user.address.city
306:     #   end
307:     #
308:     #   # SELECT "users".* FROM "users" LIMIT 5
309:     #   # SELECT "addresses".* FROM "addresses" WHERE "addresses"."id" IN (1,2,3,4,5)
310:     #
311:     # Instead of loading the 5 addresses with 5 separate queries, all addresses
312:     # are loaded with a separate query.
313:     #
314:     # Loading multiple and nested associations is possible using Hashes and Arrays,
315:     # similar to #includes:
316:     #
317:     #   User.preload(:address, friends: [:address, :followers])
318:     #   # SELECT "users".* FROM "users"
319:     #   # SELECT "addresses".* FROM "addresses" WHERE "addresses"."id" IN (1,2,3,4,5)
320:     #   # SELECT "friends".* FROM "friends" WHERE "friends"."user_id" IN (1,2,3,4,5)
321:     #   # SELECT ...

332:     # Extracts a named +association+ from the relation. The named association is first preloaded,
333:     # then the individual association records are collected from the relation. Like so:
334:     #
335:     #   account.memberships.extract_associated(:user)
336:     #   # => Returns collection of User records
337:     #
338:     # This is short-hand for:
339:     #
340:     #   account.memberships.preload(:user).collect(&:user)
341:     def extract_associated(association)
342:       preload(association).collect(&association)
343:     end

345:     # Use to indicate that the given +table_names+ are referenced by an SQL string,
346:     # and should therefore be +JOIN+ed in any query rather than loaded separately.
347:     # This method only works in conjunction with #includes.

351:     #   # Doesn't JOIN the posts table, resulting in an error.
352:     #
353:     #   User.includes(:posts).where("posts.name = 'foo'").references(:posts)
354:     #   # Query now knows the string references posts, so adds a JOIN
355:     def references(*table_names)
356:       check_if_method_has_arguments!(__callee__, table_names)
357:       spawn.references!(*table_names)

363:     end

1: # frozen_string_literal: true
2: 
3: module ActiveRecord
4:   module Encryption
5:     # Automatically expand encrypted arguments to support querying both encrypted and unencrypted data
6:     #
7:     # Active Record \Encryption supports querying the db using deterministic attributes. For example:
8:     #
9:     #   Contact.find_by(email_address: "jorge@hey.com")
10:     #
11:     # The value "jorge@hey.com" will get encrypted automatically to perform the query. But there is
12:     # a problem while the data is being encrypted. This won't work. During that time, you need these
13:     # queries to be:
14:     #
15:     #   Contact.find_by(email_address: [ "jorge@hey.com", "<encrypted jorge@hey.com>" ])
16:     #
17:     # This patches ActiveRecord to support this automatically. It addresses both:
18:     #
19:     # * ActiveRecord::Base - Used in <tt>Contact.find_by_email_address(...)</tt>
20:     # * ActiveRecord::Relation - Used in <tt>Contact.internal.find_by_email_address(...)</tt>
21:     #
22:     # This module is included if `config.active_record.encryption.extend_queries` is `true`.
23:     module ExtendedDeterministicQueries
24:       def self.install_support
25:         # ActiveRecord::Base relies on ActiveRecord::Relation (ActiveRecord::QueryMethods) but it does
26:         # some prepared statements caching. That's why we need to intercept +ActiveRecord::Base+ as soon
27:         # as it's invoked (so that the proper prepared statement is cached).
28:         ActiveRecord::Relation.prepend(RelationQueries)
29:         ActiveRecord::Base.include(CoreQueries)
30:         ActiveRecord::Encryption::EncryptedAttributeType.prepend(ExtendedEncryptableType)
31:       end
32: 
33:       # When modifying this file run performance tests in
34:       # +activerecord/test/cases/encryption/performance/extended_deterministic_queries_performance_test.rb+
35:       # to make sure performance overhead is acceptable.
36:       #
37:       # @TODO We will extend this to support previous "encryption context" versions in future iterations
38:       # @TODO Experimental. Support for every kind of query is pending
39:       # @TODO It should not patch anything if not needed (no previous schemes or no support for previous encryption schemes)
40: 
41:       module EncryptedQuery # :nodoc:
42:         class << self
43:           def process_arguments(owner, args, check_for_additional_values)
44:             owner = owner.model if owner.is_a?(Relation)
45: 
46:             return args if owner.deterministic_encrypted_attributes&.empty?
47: 
48:             if args.is_a?(Array) && (options = args.first).is_a?(Hash)
49:               options = options.transform_keys do |key|
50:                 if key.is_a?(Array)
51:                   key.map(&:to_s)
52:                 else
53:                   key.to_s
54:                 end
55:               end
56:               args[0] = options
57: 
58:               owner.deterministic_encrypted_attributes&.each do |attribute_name|
59:                 attribute_name = attribute_name.to_s
60:                 type = owner.type_for_attribute(attribute_name)
61:                 if !type.previous_types.empty? && value = options[attribute_name]
62:                   options[attribute_name] = process_encrypted_query_argument(value, check_for_additional_values, type)
63:                 end
64:               end
65:             end
66: 
67:             args
68:           end
69: 
70:           private
71:             def process_encrypted_query_argument(value, check_for_additional_values, type)
72:               return value if check_for_additional_values && value.is_a?(Array) && value.last.is_a?(AdditionalValue)
73: 
74:               case value
75:               when String, Array
76:                 list = Array(value)
77:                 list + list.flat_map do |each_value|
78:                   if check_for_additional_values && each_value.is_a?(AdditionalValue)
79:                     each_value
80:                   else
81:                     additional_values_for(each_value, type)
82:                   end
83:                 end
84:               else
85:                 value
86:               end
87:             end
88: 
89:             def additional_values_for(value, type)
90:               type.previous_types.collect do |additional_type|
91:                 AdditionalValue.new(value, additional_type)
92:               end
93:             end
94:         end
95:       end
96: 
97:       module RelationQueries
98:         def where(*args)
99:           super(*EncryptedQuery.process_arguments(self, args, true))
100:         end
101: 
102:         def exists?(*args)
103:           super(*EncryptedQuery.process_arguments(self, args, true))
104:         end
105: 
106:         def scope_for_create
107:           return super unless model.deterministic_encrypted_attributes&.any?
108: 
109:           scope_attributes = super
110:           wheres = where_values_hash
111: 
112:           model.deterministic_encrypted_attributes.each do |attribute_name|
113:             attribute_name = attribute_name.to_s
114:             values = wheres[attribute_name]
115:             if values.is_a?(Array) && values[1..].all?(AdditionalValue)
116:               scope_attributes[attribute_name] = values.first
117:             end
118:           end
119: 
120:           scope_attributes
121:         end
122:       end
123: 
124:       module CoreQueries
125:         extend ActiveSupport::Concern
126: 
127:         class_methods do
128:           def find_by(*args)
129:             super(*EncryptedQuery.process_arguments(self, args, false))
130:           end
131:         end
132:       end
133: 
134:       class AdditionalValue
135:         attr_reader :value, :type
136: 
137:         def initialize(value, type)
138:           @type = type
139:           @value = process(value)
140:         end
141: 
142:         private
143:           def process(value)
144:             type.serialize(value)
145:           end
146:       end
147: 
148:       module ExtendedEncryptableType
149:         def serialize(data)
150:           if data.is_a?(AdditionalValue)
151:             data.value
152:           else
153:             super
154:           end
155:         end
156:       end
157:     end
158:   end
159: end

(End of file - total 159 lines)

1: # frozen_string_literal: true
2: 
3: module ActiveSupport
4:   module EnumerableCoreExt # :nodoc:
5:     module Constants
6:       private
7:         def const_missing(name)
8:           if name == :SoleItemExpectedError
9:             ::ActiveSupport::EnumerableCoreExt::SoleItemExpectedError
10:           else
11:             super
12:           end
13:         end
14:     end
15:   end
16: end
17: 
18: module Enumerable
19:   # Error generated by +sole+ when called on an enumerable that doesn't have
20:   # exactly one item.
21:   class SoleItemExpectedError < StandardError; end
22: 
23:   # HACK: For performance reasons, Enumerable shouldn't have any constants of its own.
24:   # So we move SoleItemExpectedError into ActiveSupport::EnumerableCoreExt.
25:   ActiveSupport::EnumerableCoreExt::SoleItemExpectedError = remove_const(:SoleItemExpectedError)
26:   singleton_class.prepend(ActiveSupport::EnumerableCoreExt::Constants)
27: 
28:   # Calculates the minimum from the extracted elements.
29:   #
30:   #   payments = [Payment.new(5), Payment.new(15), Payment.new(10)]
31:   #   payments.minimum(:price) # => 5
32:   def minimum(key)
33:     map(&key).min
34:   end
35: 
36:   # Calculates the maximum from the extracted elements.
37:   #
38:   #   payments = [Payment.new(5), Payment.new(15), Payment.new(10)]
39:   #   payments.maximum(:price) # => 15
40:   def maximum(key)
41:     map(&key).max
42:   end
43: 
44:   # Convert an enumerable to a hash, using the block result as the key and the
45:   # element as the value.
46:   #
47:   #   people.index_by(&:login)
48:   #   # => { "nextangle" => <Person ...>, "chade-" => <Person ...>, ...}
49:   #
50:   #   people.index_by { |person| "#{person.first_name} #{person.last_name}" }
51:   #   # => { "Chade- Fowlersburg-e" => <Person ...>, "David Heinemeier Hansson" => <Person ...>, ...}
52:   def index_by
53:     if block_given?
54:       result = {}
55:       each { |elem| result[yield(elem)] = elem }
56:       result
57:     else
58:       to_enum(:index_by) { size if respond_to?(:size) }
59:     end
60:   end
61: 
62:   # Convert an enumerable to a hash, using the element as the key and the block
63:   # result as the value.
64:   #
65:   #   post = Post.new(title: "hey there", body: "what's up?")
66:   #
67:   #   %i( title body ).index_with { |attr_name| post.public_send(attr_name) }
68:   #   # => { title: "hey there", body: "what's up?" }
69:   #
70:   # If an argument is passed instead of a block, it will be used as the value
71:   # for all elements:
72:   #
73:   #   %i( created_at updated_at ).index_with(Time.now)
74:   #   # => { created_at: 2020-03-09 22:31:47, updated_at: 2020-03-09 22:31:47 }
75:   def index_with(default = (no_default = true))
76:     if block_given?
77:       result = {}
78:       each { |elem| result[elem] = yield(elem) }
79:       result
80:     elsif no_default

1: # frozen_string_literal: true
2: 
3: module ActiveRecord
4:   module Associations
5:     # = Active Record Collection Proxy
6:     #
7:     # Collection proxies in Active Record are middlemen between an
8:     # <tt>association</tt>, and its <tt>target</tt> result set.
9:     #
10:     # For example, given
11:     #
12:     #   class Blog < ActiveRecord::Base
13:     #     has_many :posts
14:     #   end
15:     #
16:     #   blog = Blog.first
17:     #
18:     # The collection proxy returned by <tt>blog.posts</tt> is built from a
19:     # <tt>:has_many</tt> <tt>association</tt>, and delegates to a collection
20:     # of posts as the <tt>target</tt>.
21:     #
22:     # This class delegates unknown methods to the <tt>association</tt>'s
23:     # relation class via a delegate cache.
24:     #
25:     # The <tt>target</tt> result set is not loaded until needed. For example,
26:     #
27:     #   blog.posts.count
28:     #
29:     # is computed directly through SQL and does not trigger by itself the
30:     # instantiation of the actual post records.
31:     class CollectionProxy < Relation
32:       def initialize(klass, association, **) # :nodoc:
33:         @association = association
34:         super klass
35: 
36:         extensions = association.extensions
37:         extend(*extensions) if extensions.any?
38:       end
39: 
40:       def target
41:         @association.target
42:       end
43: 
44:       def load_target
45:         @association.load_target
46:       end
47: 
48:       # Returns +true+ if the association has been loaded, otherwise +false+.
49:       #
50:       #   person.pets.loaded? # => false
51:       #   person.pets.records
52:       #   person.pets.loaded? # => true
53:       def loaded?
54:         @association.loaded?
55:       end
56:       alias :loaded :loaded?
57: 
58:       ##
59:       # :method: select
60:       #
61:       # :call-seq:
62:       #   select(*fields, &block)
63:       #
64:       # Works in two ways.
65:       #
66:       # *First:* Specify a subset of fields to be selected from the result set.
67:       #
68:       #   class Person < ActiveRecord::Base
69:       #     has_many :pets
70:       #   end
71:       #
72:       #   person.pets
73:       #   # => [
74:       #   #       #<Pet id: 1, name: "Fancy-Fancy", person_id: 1>,

76:       #   #       #<Pet id: 3, name: "Choo-Choo", person_id: 1>

3: module ActiveRecord
4:   class DisableJoinsAssociationRelation < Relation # :nodoc:
5:     attr_reader :ids, :key
6: 
7:     def initialize(klass, key, ids)
8:       @ids = ids.uniq
9:       @key = key
10:       super(klass)
11:     end
12: 
13:     def limit(value)
14:       records.take(value)
15:     end
16: 
17:     def first(limit = nil)
18:       if limit
19:         records.limit(limit).first
20:       else
21:         records.first
22:       end
23:     end
24: 
25:     def load
26:       super
27:       records = @records
28: 
29:       records_by_id = records.group_by do |record|
30:         if key.is_a?(Array)
31:           key.map { |column| record[column] }
32:         else
33:           record[key]
34:         end
35:       end
36: 
37:       records = ids.flat_map { |id| records_by_id[id] }
38:       records.compact!
39: 
40:       @records = records
41:     end
42:   end
43: end

2: 
3: module ActiveRecord
4:   module Associations
5:     class DisableJoinsAssociationScope < AssociationScope # :nodoc:
6:       def scope(association)
7:         source_reflection = association.reflection
8:         owner = association.owner
9:         unscoped = association.klass.unscoped
10:         reverse_chain = get_chain(source_reflection, association, unscoped.alias_tracker).reverse
11: 
12:         last_reflection, last_ordered, last_join_ids = last_scope_chain(reverse_chain, owner)
13: 
14:         add_constraints(last_reflection, last_reflection.join_primary_key, last_join_ids, owner, last_ordered)
15:       end
16: 
17:       private
18:         def last_scope_chain(reverse_chain, owner)
19:           first_item = reverse_chain.shift
20:           first_scope = [first_item, false, [owner._read_attribute(first_item.join_foreign_key)]]
21:

private
      def resolve_config_for_connection(config_or_env)
        raise "Anonymous class is not allowed." unless name

        connection_name = primary_class? ? Base.name : name
        self.connection_specification_name = connection_name

        Base.configurations.resolve(config_or_env)
      end

      def with_role_and_shard(role, shard, prevent_writes)
        append_to_connected_to_stack(role: role, shard: shard, prevent_writes: prevent_writes, klasses: [self])
        begin
          return_value = yield
          return_value.load if return_value.is_a? ActiveRecord::Relation
          return_value
        ensure
          self.connected_to_stack.pop
        end
      end

      def append_to_connected_to_stack(entry)
        if entry[:shard].present? && entry[:klasses].any?(&:shard_swapping_prohibited?)
          raise ShardSwapProhibitedError, "cannot swap `shard` while shard swapping is prohibited."
        end

        connected_to_stack << entry
      end
  end
end
